title: 1 Corinthians 8
subtitle: Food Offered to Idols: Knowledge and Conscience

sections.header: Verses 1–6 — "Knowledge Puffs Up, but Love Builds Up"

heb.paragraph: The noun gnōsis (knowledge) is a key term in the Corinthian correspondence, appearing 16 times in 1–2 Corinthians (compared to only 7 times in all other Pauline letters). In this context the ‘knowledge’ in question is the theological insight that idols are nothing and there is only one God (v. 4). Paul does not dispute the truth of this knowledge but challenges its sufficiency: knowledge alone ‘puffs up’ (physioō), while love ‘builds up’ (oikodomei). The contrast echoes the wisdom/cross dialectic of chapters 1–2: correct theology without cruciform love is as empty as eloquence without the cross.

The noun syneidēsis (conscience) appears eight times in chapters 8–10, becoming the ethical centre of the idol-food discussion. In Greco-Roman moral philosophy, syneidēsis denoted the inner moral witness that either accuses or approves one’s conduct. Paul recognises that consciences differ: the ‘strong’ have knowledge and eat freely; the ‘weak’ lack this knowledge and eat with a defiled conscience. The ethical question is not ‘what do I know?’ but ‘what does my action do to my brother’s or sister’s conscience?’

refs.note: Paul addresses a parallel dispute in Rome over food and observance of days, using the same strong/weak framework and concluding that love must constrain liberty.
The Jerusalem Council’s prohibition of food offered to idols provides the earlier apostolic ruling that forms the backdrop to Paul’s discussion.
The Shema (‘Hear, O Israel: the Lord our God, the Lord is one’) stands behind the monotheistic confession of v. 6 that Paul shares with the ‘knowledgeable.’

notes.note: Paul’s opening thesis is programmatic: knowledge puffs up, but love builds up. The Corinthians’ problem is not wrong knowledge but knowledge deployed without love. The one who thinks they know something ‘does not yet know as they ought to know’ (v. 2). True knowledge is relational: the one who loves God ‘is known by God’ (v. 3).

Paul affirms the theological content of the Corinthians’ knowledge: an idol is nothing; there is only one God. But he extends the monotheistic confession christologically: there is one God, the Father, from whom all things came; and one Lord, Jesus Christ, through whom all things came. This christological monotheism — placing Christ within the identity of the one God — is one of the most significant theological statements in the NT.

Calvin warns that knowledge without love is a deadly spiritual pathology. The puffed-up person may have correct doctrine but lacks the charity that alone gives knowledge its proper function. Calvin draws the application: theology must serve the church, not the theologian’s ego.

Calvin reads v. 6 as a compressed Trinitarian formula: the Father as source (ex hou ta panta) and the Son as agent (di hou ta panta). Christ’s mediatorial role in creation and redemption is affirmed in a single breathtaking sentence.

The phrase peri de tōn eidōlothytōn (‘now about food sacrificed to idols’) introduces a new section responding to the Corinthians’ letter. The term eidōlothyton (idol-sacrifice) is a Jewish coinage combining eidōlon (‘idol’) and thytos (‘sacrificed’).

The bipartite formula (one God, one Lord) adapts the Jewish Shema (Deut 6:4), splitting the divine identity between the Father and Christ. The prepositions are significant: ek (‘from’) designates the Father as source; dia (‘through’) designates Christ as agent.

Fee argues that the knowledge/love contrast is the hermeneutical key to chapters 8–10. The ‘knowledgeable’ Corinthians are theologically correct but ethically wrong. Their error is the same one exposed in chapters 1–4: they prioritise individual gnosis over communal agape.

Fee stresses that v. 6 is not a credal formula but a christological reworking of the Shema. By placing Christ alongside the Father within the one-God confession, Paul makes the most explicit claim for Christ’s divine status in the entire letter. This is not binitarianism but genuine christological monotheism.

Thiselton argues that Paul’s critique targets not knowledge itself but the speech-act of claiming knowledge without love. ‘We all possess knowledge’ (v. 1) is another Corinthian slogan that Paul quotes and qualifies. True knowledge is not propositional mastery but relational — being known by God (v. 3).

Thiselton identifies v. 6 as a ‘christological redefinition of monotheism’: the inclusion of Christ within the divine identity marked by the Shema is among the earliest and most significant developments in NT Christology. The verse is both confessional and polemical, positioning Christian monotheism over against the ‘many gods and many lords’ of Corinthian paganism.

hist.context: Paul introduces another topic from the Corinthians’ letter (peri de, ‘now about,’ v. 1): food sacrificed to idols (eidōlothyta). In Roman Corinth, virtually all meat available in the marketplace (macellum) had been offered to pagan deities in temple rituals before being sold. Social life — guild meetings, civic celebrations, family occasions — routinely took place in temple dining rooms. For Corinthian Christians, avoiding idol-food entirely would mean near-total social isolation and economic disadvantage. The ‘knowledgeable’ faction argued that since idols are nothing and there is only one God, eating such food was harmless. Paul agrees with the theological premise but challenges its ethical application.

sections.header: Verses 7–13 — "Do Not Cause Your Brother or Sister to Fall"

heb.paragraph: The noun proskomma (stumbling block) in v. 9 denotes an obstacle placed in someone’s path that causes them to trip. Paul warns the ‘knowledgeable’ that their exercise of liberty (exousia) could become a proskomma for the ‘weak’ — not merely offending their sensibilities but actively causing them to act against their conscience (v. 10), which Paul regards as spiritual destruction. The concept echoes Jesus’ warning about causing ‘little ones’ to stumble (Matt 18:6) and will be developed further in Rom 14:13.

refs.note: Jesus’ severe warning about causing ‘little ones’ to stumble provides the dominical background for Paul’s argument about the weak brother.
Paul’s parallel discussion of food scruples in Rome concludes with the same principle: do not destroy your brother or sister for the sake of food.
Paul’s direct statement: ‘if your brother or sister is distressed because of what you eat, you are no longer acting in love’ parallels the ethical logic of 1 Cor 8.

notes.note: Not everyone has the mature knowledge that idols are nothing. Some recent converts still associate idol-food with actual pagan worship. Their conscience is ‘weak’ — not in the sense of moral feebleness but of insufficient theological understanding. Eating with a defiled conscience defiles the person. Food itself is morally neutral (v. 8), but the act of eating against one’s conscience is not.

The ‘knowledgeable’ believer’s right (exousia) to eat in a temple dining room is real but limited by love. If exercising that right causes a ‘weak’ believer to act against conscience, the ‘knowledgeable’ believer is sinning against Christ. Paul’s logic is stunning: injuring a brother for whom Christ died is an offence against Christ himself.

Paul’s conclusion is unambiguous: to wound the weak conscience is to sin against Christ. Therefore Paul will voluntarily restrict his own freedom: ‘if what I eat causes my brother or sister to fall into sin, I will never eat meat again.’ This sets up the extended argument of chapter 9, where Paul models the voluntary surrender of apostolic rights.

Calvin argues that the strong must accommodate the weak not out of mere courtesy but out of genuine love. Knowledge that fails to consider the weaker brother is not true knowledge but spiritual pride. Calvin warns that the public exercise of liberty in a temple dining room is particularly dangerous because it provides a visible model that the weak may imitate against their conscience.

Calvin drives home the christological dimension: the weak brother is one ‘for whom Christ died.’ To despise the weak is to despise the price Christ paid for them. Calvin concludes that no exercise of liberty is worth the destruction of a soul purchased by Christ’s blood.

The phrase en eidōleiō katakeimenon (‘reclining in an idol’s temple’) refers to the widespread practice of dining in temple precincts. Archaeological evidence from Corinth confirms numerous temple dining rooms used for sacrificial meals, guild banquets, and civic celebrations.

The phrase apollytai ho asthenōn (‘the weak person is destroyed’) uses the present middle/passive of apollymi, a strong term for eschatological ruin. Paul does not speak of mere offence but of spiritual destruction.

Fee argues that the ‘weak’ are not merely scrupulous but spiritually endangered: their recent conversion from idolatry means that eating in a temple could draw them back into genuine pagan worship. The issue is not squeamishness but apostasy. Paul’s concern is soteriological, not sentimental.

Fee stresses that Paul’s personal example (‘I will never eat meat again’) is not hypothetical but programmatic: it introduces chapter 9, where Paul demonstrates that he has already surrendered far greater rights than food for the sake of the gospel.

Thiselton draws on the concept of ‘performative contradiction’: the ‘knowledgeable’ believer’s claim to possess love (implied by 8:1) is contradicted by the performative effect of their action, which destroys the brother. The speech-act of claiming love is vitiated by the embodied act of eating in a temple.

Thiselton highlights the christological grounding of Paul’s ethic: the weak person is defined not by their weakness but by Christ’s death for them. The strong person’s liberty is bounded by the same cross that liberated them. Paul’s voluntary renunciation (v. 13) embodies the cruciform pattern of chapters 1–4.

hist.context: Paul now addresses the practical problem: not everyone in the church possesses the same theological knowledge. Some believers, recently converted from paganism, still associate idol-food with actual demonic worship. If they see a ‘knowledgeable’ fellow believer eating in a temple dining room (v. 10), they may be emboldened to eat despite a conflicted conscience — and this, Paul argues, constitutes their destruction. The archaeological evidence from Corinth confirms the prevalence of temple dining: the Asclepion, the Demeter sanctuary, and other temples had dining rooms where worshippers ate meals after sacrifices. For the ‘weak’ Corinthian, these spaces were saturated with spiritual significance that could not simply be overridden by theological knowledge.

themes.note: Worship dominates: the entire discussion concerns the intersection of Christian faith with pagan worship practices. Faith scores highly with the christological monotheism of v. 6. Mercy rises through Paul’s concern for the vulnerable brother. Mission is present in the call to live in a way that builds up rather than destroys.

rows.label: vv.7–13
rows.text: The Weak Conscience and the Limits of Liberty

lit.note: Chapter 8 opens the idol-food discussion (chs. 8–10) by establishing the governing principle: knowledge must be constrained by love. The theological insight that idols are nothing (correct) must yield to the pastoral reality that the weak brother’s conscience can be destroyed. Paul’s voluntary self-limitation (v. 13) anticipates the extended argument of chapter 9.

hebtext.note: The noun gnōsis (knowledge) is a key term in the Corinthian correspondence, appearing 16 times in 1–2 Corinthians (compared to only 7 times in all other Pauline letters). In this context the ‘knowledge’ in question is the theological insight that idols are nothing and there is only one God (v. 4). ppl.name: Paul
ppl.role: Apostle to the Gentiles; author of thirteen NT letters
ppl.text: Paul (Saul of Tarsus) is introduced as a persecutor at Stephen’s stoning (Acts 7:58) and is dramatically converted on the Damascus road (Acts 9). He dominates Acts 13–28 across three missionary journeys, planting churches from Cyprus to Greece to Rome. His final journey to Rome as a prisoner frames Acts’ theological climax: the gospel reaching the empire’s capital, "boldly and without hindrance" (Acts 28:31).

ppl.name: Christ
ppl.text: For Corinthian Christians, avoiding idol-food entirely would mean near-total social isolation and economic disadvantage…

trans.title: Comparing translations in Food Offered to Idols: Knowledge and Conscience

translations.text: But not everyone possesses this knowledge. Some people are still so accustomed to idols that when they eat sacrificial food they think of it as having been sacrificed to a god, and since their conscience is weak, it is defiled.
However, not all possess this knowledge. But some, through former association with idols, eat food as really offered to an idol, and their conscience, being weak, is defiled.
yet for us there is but one God, the Father, from whom all things came and for whom we live; and there is but one Lord, Jesus Christ, through whom all things came and through whom we live.
yet for us there is one God, the Father, from whom are all things and for whom we exist, and one Lord, Jesus Christ, through whom are all things and through whom we exist.

Emphasises historical-critical, literary, or text-critical analysis.
Reads Food Offered to Idols within the canonical framework of fulfilled prophecy and covenant theology.
Represented by Calvin, MacArthur, and the evangelical tradition.
Both readings engage the text seriously; they differ primarily in their hermeneutical starting points and assumptions about authorship and historical context.

thread.text: Paul addresses a parallel dispute in Rome over food and observance of days, using the same strong/weak framework and concluding that love must constrain liberty.
The Jerusalem Council’s prohibition of food offered to idols provides the earlier apostolic ruling that forms the backdrop to Paul’s discussion.
The Shema (‘Hear, O Israel: the Lord our God, the Lord is one’) stands behind the monotheistic confession of v. 6 that Paul shares with the ‘knowledgeable.’
Jesus’ severe warning about causing ‘little ones’ to stumble provides the dominical background for Paul’s argument about the weak brother.
Paul’s parallel discussion of food scruples in Rome concludes with the same principle: do not destroy your brother or sister for the sake of food.
Paul’s direct statement: ‘if your brother or sister is distressed because of what you eat, you are no longer acting in love’ parallels the ethical logic of 1 Cor 8.

discourse.thesis: Knowledge without love destroys — those who know that idols are nothing must limit their freedom out of love for weaker brothers whose conscience would be wounded by seeing them eat at idol feasts.

nodes.text: On food offered to idols — 'we all possess knowledge.' Knowledge (γνῶσις) puffs up (φυσιοῖ); love builds up (οἰκοδομεῖ). If anyone imagines he knows something, he has not yet known as he ought to know. But if anyone loves God, this person is known by God. The knowledge/love contrast will govern the entire argument.

The 'strong' position: an idol has no real existence; there is no God but one. Although there are 'gods' and 'lords' — yet for us there is one God, the Father, from whom are all things and for whom we exist, and one Lord Jesus Christ, through whom are all things and through whom we exist. Theologically correct — but not the end of the argument.

Not everyone has this knowledge. Some, accustomed to idols until now, eat this food as really offered to an idol — and their conscience, being weak (ἀσθενής), is defiled. Food will not commend us to God — but take care that this right (ἐξουσία) of yours does not somehow become a stumbling block to the weak.

children.text: The person of weak conscience is a brother for whom Christ died — by wounding their conscience you sin against Christ. Therefore if food makes my brother stumble, I will never eat meat (οὐ μὴ φάγω κρέα εἰς τὸν αἰῶνα), lest I cause my brother to stumble. The infinite scope of the sacrifice: knowledge that could justify eating is surrendered permanently for the sake of a brother.

discourse.note: 1 Corinthians 8 introduces the principle that governs chs. 8-10: the 'strong' are not wrong about their theology — idols are nothing — but they are wrong about their responsibility. Freedom is real but must be exercised with love for the weaker sibling. Paul will illustrate this principle with his own apostolic example in ch. 9 before returning to the idol-food question in ch. 10.

coaching.tip: 'Knowledge puffs up, but love builds up' (v. 1). The Corinthians knew idols were nothing — correct theology. But they ate idol-meat without regard for weaker consciences. Knowledge without love inflates; love applies knowledge carefully. Being right isn't enough if being right wounds brothers.

'If food makes my brother stumble, I will never eat meat, lest I make my brother stumble' (v. 13). Paul's principle: voluntary restriction for others' sake. Freedom isn't maximizing personal liberty but considering communal impact. Rights exist to be waived when love requires. This isn't legalism but love.
